question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the always-nil MempoolResponse from Mempool() to cause a Rosetta data pipeline that uses mempool data to build transaction graphs to produce an empty graph, leading to incorrect reconciliation of Hashgraph history and masking unauthorized transfers?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: mempoolAPIService] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the zero-value mempoolAPIService struct (no fields, no state) to bypass any expected mempool-based replay protection, since the service cannot track previously seen transaction IDs, allowing replay of previously confirmed Hashgraph transactions through the construction pipeline?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the unconditional ErrNotImplemented from Mempool() to cause a Rosetta block-builder integration to skip mempool-based fee estimation, submit transactions with artificially low fees that get reordered by Hashgraph nodes, and thereby manipulate the effective ordering of transactions in confirmed history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: MempoolTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of network-identifier validation in MempoolTransaction() (request discarded) to send cross-network transaction identifiers that, when the error response is used by a client to infer network state, cause the client to query the wrong network's history and produce a corrupted view of Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of request-body size limits in Mempool() (body discarded but parsed by HTTP layer) to send multi-megabyte NetworkRequest payloads that exhaust the HTTP server's memory, causing OOM conditions that crash the history-serving components of the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: MempoolTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of request-body size limits in MempoolTransaction() to send multi-megabyte MempoolTransactionRequest payloads, causing the HTTP server to allocate unbounded memory during JSON deserialization before the handler discards the request, leading to OOM crashes that interrupt Hashgraph history availability?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ErrNotImplemented (code 111) response from Mempool() to enumerate which Rosetta endpoints are implemented vs. not, building a precise attack surface map of the mirror node that identifies the weakest history-manipulation entry points?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: MempoolTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the consistent ErrNotImplemented response from MempoolTransaction() to confirm that transaction existence in the mempool is never checked, then use the /construction/submit endpoint to submit a transaction with a forged transaction ID that collides with a historical Hashgraph transaction, causing history integrity violations?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of IP-based or token-based rate limiting on the Mempool() handler (no fields on mempoolAPIService) to launch a sustained request flood that starves the goroutine pool, preventing the mirror node from processing new Hashgraph consensus records and creating gaps in the confirmed history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: MempoolTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of rate limiting on MempoolTransaction() to send thousands of concurrent requests, exhausting the HTTP server's connection slots and preventing legitimate clients from querying confirmed Hashgraph history through other endpoints on the same server?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that Mempool() returns ErrNotImplemented regardless of the network identifier in the request, to use the endpoint as a side-channel to confirm that the mirror node is processing a specific Hashgraph network, enabling targeted attacks on that network's history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: MempoolTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that MempoolTransaction() never validates the TransactionIdentifier in the request to submit requests with transaction hashes that match historical Hashgraph transactions, using the ErrNotImplemented response to confirm the endpoint is live and then pivot to replaying those historical transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the nil return value from Mempool() to cause a Rosetta client's mempool-to-block reconciliation logic to treat all pending transactions as confirmed, leading to premature finalization of unconfirmed Hashgraph state and corrupting the historical record?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: mempoolAPIService] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the empty mempoolAPIService struct (no nonce or sequence tracking) to submit transactions with the same valid_start_nanos through the construction pipeline, bypassing any mempool-level deduplication that would normally prevent duplicate entries in Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the discarded NetworkRequest in Mempool() to send requests with a network identifier pointing to a different shard or realm, and use the uniform ErrNotImplemented response to infer that no cross-shard validation exists, enabling cross-shard history manipulation attacks?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: MempoolTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the discarded MempoolTransactionRequest in MempoolTransaction() to send requests with Unicode or null-byte-injected transaction identifiers, and because no sanitization occurs before the error is returned, cause log injection in any middleware that logs the raw request, polluting audit logs of Hashgraph history events?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ErrNotImplemented response from Mempool() to cause a Rosetta block-synchronization daemon to enter an infinite retry loop (despite retriable: false, some clients ignore this), consuming all available CPU and preventing the daemon from writing new Hashgraph consensus records to the history store?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: MempoolTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ErrNotImplemented response from MempoolTransaction() to cause a Rosetta reconciliation tool to skip transaction-existence verification, allowing the attacker to submit a transaction that references a non-existent prior transaction in Hashgraph history, creating a dangling reference that corrupts the history graph?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any CORS or origin validation on the Mempool() endpoint (no fields on mempoolAPIService) to make cross-origin requests from a malicious web page, using the ErrNotImplemented response to confirm the mirror node's presence and then launching CSRF-based history-manipulation attacks against authenticated users?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: MempoolTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any CORS or origin validation on MempoolTransaction() to make cross-origin probing requests, confirming the mirror node's network configuration and using that information to craft targeted transaction-replay attacks against Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that Mempool() never checks whether the service is in online or offline mode (unlike other services that check IsOnline()), to send mempool requests to an offline node and use the ErrNotImplemented response to confirm offline status, then exploit the offline mode's relaxed validation to manipulate transaction construction for later history injection?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: MempoolTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that MempoolTransaction() never checks IsOnline() (unlike BaseService methods) to send requests to an offline node, confirming offline mode and then using the construction endpoints' offline-mode behavior to craft unsigned transactions that, when submitted later, manipulate Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the always-nil MempoolResponse from Mempool() to cause a Rosetta data indexer to record an empty mempool snapshot at every polling interval, creating a false historical record that shows no pending transactions were ever present, which could be used to dispute the legitimacy of confirmed Hashgraph transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: MempoolTransaction()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the always-nil MempoolTransactionResponse from MempoolTransaction() to cause a Rosetta client to record that a specific transaction was never in the mempool, creating a false audit trail that contradicts the confirmed Hashgraph history and enabling disputes about transaction legitimacy?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/mempool_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Mempool()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of any request deduplication in Mempool() (stateless mempoolAPIService) to send identical requests in rapid succession, and if any upstream caching layer caches the ErrNotImplemented response with a long TTL, cause legitimate mempool queries to receive stale cached errors even if the endpoint is later implemented, creating a persistent denial of mempool visibility?
